examples.explanation: Vertices: [0,1]. Permutations: [0,1]: dist=dist[0][1]=5. [1,0]: dist=dist[1][0]=5. Minimum is 5.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n! * n)
approaches.summary: Generate all permutations of vertices to explore every possible Hamiltonian path, compute the total distance for each, and track the minimum. Use this for small n (e.g., n <= 10) due to factorial growth.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Shortest Hamiltonian Path
ladders.desc: Find the shortest Hamiltonian path in a complete weighted graph by generating all permutations of vertices.
ladders.explanation: The Hamiltonian path problem requires visiting each vertex exactly once. In a complete weighted graph with n vertices, the brute force approach explores every possible ordering of vertices to find the path with minimum total weight. The intuition is that with small n (≤10), we can exhaustively check all n! permutations. For each permutation, we sum the edge weights between consecutive vertices (using the distance matrix) and track the minimum. This guarantees finding the optimal solution but becomes infeasible for larger n due to factorial growth. The algorithm: 1) Initialize min_distance = infinity. 2) Generate all permutations of vertices [0,1,...,n-1]. 3) For each permutation, compute total distance by summing dist[perm[i]][perm[i+1]] for i=0 to n-2. 4) Update min_distance if current total is smaller. 5) Return min_distance. Time: O(n! * n) for n! permutations and O(n) to compute each path's distance. Space: O(n) for storing permutations and intermediate sums. Connection to L1: This uses the permutation generation from L1 as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling n=1 (should return 0), forgetting to check all permutations, or incorrect distance indexing. Real code pattern: Use itertools.permutations in Python or recursive backtracking.

examples.trace: Vertices: [0,1]. Permutations: [0,1]: dist=dist[0][1]=5. [1,0]: dist=dist[1][0]=5. Minimum is 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Generating All Permutations
ladders.desc: Generate all possible orderings of n distinct vertices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine creates every permutation of vertices [0,1,...,n-1], which is essential for exploring all Hamiltonian paths. The intuition is that a Hamiltonian path is an ordering of vertices, so we need all n! orderings. For small n, we can use recursion with backtracking: start with an empty list, recursively add each unused vertex until all vertices are used, then record the permutation. Alternatively, use iterative methods like Heap's algorithm or library functions. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize result list. 2) Define recursive function backtrack(path, used): if path length = n, add copy to result. Else, for each vertex i not in used: add i to path, mark i as used, recurse, then backtrack. 3) Call backtrack([], set()). Time: O(n! * n) to generate n! permutations each of length n. Space: O(n! * n) to store all permutations, but can be optimized to O(n) by generating on-the-fly. Connection to L0: This provides the permutations needed to compute path distances. Common mistakes: Not copying paths before adding to result (leading to reference issues), or inefficient generation for n>10. Real code pattern: Use recursion with a visited set or itertools.permutations.

examples.trace: Start backtrack: path=[], used={}. Try i=0: path=[0], used={0}. Recurse: try i=1: path=[0,1], used={0,1}. Recurse: try i=2: path=[0,1,2], used={0,1,2} -> add [0,1,2]. Backtrack: remove 2, try next i. No more, backtrack remove 1, try i=2: path=[0,2], used={0,2}. Recurse: try i=1: path=[0,2,1] -> add [0,2,1]. Continue similarly for starting with i=1 and i=2.
Start: path=[], used={}. Try i=0: path=[0], used={0}. Recurse: try i=1: path=[0,1] -> add [0,1]. Backtrack, try i=1 first: path=[1], used={1}. Recurse: try i=0: path=[1,0] -> add [1,0].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Computing Path Distance from a Permutation
ladders.desc: Given a permutation of vertices and a distance matrix, compute the total edge weight along the path.
ladders.explanation: This computes the sum of distances between consecutive vertices in a permutation, representing a Hamiltonian path. The intuition is that a path's total weight is the cumulative cost of moving from one vertex to the next in the given order. For a permutation P of length n, the distance is Σ_{i=0}^{n-2} dist[P[i]][P[i+1]]. This assumes a complete graph where dist[i][j] is the weight from i to j. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize total = 0. 2) For i from 0 to n-2: total += dist[P[i]][P[i+1]]. 3) Return total. Time: O(n) for iterating through the permutation. Space: O(1) aside from input. Connection to L1: This function is applied to each permutation generated. Common mistakes: Off-by-one errors (e.g., going to n-1 instead of n-2), assuming symmetric distances (not required but common), or not handling n=1 (path distance should be 0). Real code pattern: Simple loop over the permutation list.

examples.trace: n=3. i=0: dist[1][0]=1, total=1. i=1: dist[0][2]=2, total=1+2=3. Return 3.
n=2. i=0: dist[0][1]=5, total=5. Return 5.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over Array Pairs
ladders.desc: Process consecutive pairs of elements in an array to compute a cumulative sum.
ladders.explanation: This involves traversing an array and performing an operation on each adjacent pair of elements. The intuition is that many path or sequence problems require aggregating values between successive items. For an array A of length m, we consider pairs (A[i], A[i+1]) for i from 0 to m-2. This is done using a loop that accesses current and next elements. Step-by-step: 1) Initialize result = 0 or appropriate identity. 2) For i in range(0, len(A)-1): compute operation on A[i] and A[i+1], update result. 3) Return result. Time: O(m) for linear scan. Space: O(1). Connection to L2: This pattern is used to sum distances between consecutive vertices in a permutation. Common mistakes: Incorrect loop bounds causing index out-of-range, not handling empty or single-element arrays, or misunderstanding pair ordering. Real code pattern: for i in range(len(A)-1): val = f(A[i], A[i+1]).

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Accessing 2D Array Elements
ladders.desc: Retrieve values from a 2D array (matrix) using row and column indices.
ladders.explanation: This involves accessing an element in a 2D array given its row i and column j, typically as dist[i][j]. The intuition is that a distance matrix stores weights between vertices, where dist[i][j] represents the cost from vertex i to vertex j. In programming, a 2D array is a list of lists: dist[i] is the i-th row, and dist[i][j] is the j-th element in that row. Step-by-step: 1) Ensure indices are within bounds: 0 ≤ i,j < n. 2) Access value: val = dist[i][j]. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used in the pair iteration to get distances between vertices. Common mistakes: Index errors from off-by-one, confusing row/column order, or assuming matrix symmetry. Real code pattern: Direct indexing in nested lists.

examples.trace: dist is 3x3. dist[1] is [3,4,5]. dist[1][2] is the element at index 2 in that row: 5.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understand basic array data structure and how to access elements by position.
ladders.explanation: An array is a collection of elements stored in contiguous memory, accessible by an index starting from 0. The intuition is that it allows efficient random access to any element based on its position. For a 1D array A of length m, A[i] retrieves the element at index i (0 ≤ i < m). This is fundamental because the distance matrix is a 2D array (array of arrays), and vertices are represented as indices. Step-by-step: 1) Define array: e.g., A = [10, 20, 30]. 2) Access: A[0] = 10, A[1] = 20, A[2] = 30. Time: O(1) for access. Space: O(m) to store array. Connection to L4: This basic skill is extended to 2D arrays. Common mistakes: Using 1-based indexing (common in math but not programming), index out-of-range errors, or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: In Python, lists are used as arrays: A = [1,2,3]; x = A[0].

examples.trace: Array A has elements: index 0:5, index 1:8, index 2:3. A[1] accesses element at index 1: 8.
Array A has one element at index 0: 100. A[0] returns 100.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with Bitmasking O(n^2 * 2^n)
approaches.summary: Use DP where dp[mask][i] stores the shortest path cost to visit vertices in mask ending at vertex i, iterating over subsets to build solutions. This is the standard approach for medium-sized n (e.g., n <= 20).

ladders.title: Full Problem — Shortest Hamiltonian Path with DP and Bitmasking
ladders.desc: Find the minimum cost to visit all vertices exactly once in a weighted graph using DP with bitmasking.
ladders.explanation: The Hamiltonian path problem requires visiting every vertex exactly once. For n vertices, we need to consider all permutations (n! possibilities), which is infeasible for n>10. The DP with bitmasking approach reduces this to O(n² * 2ⁿ) by representing visited vertices as a bitmask (integer where bit i=1 means vertex i visited). dp[mask][i] stores the minimum cost to visit vertices in mask ending at vertex i. We initialize dp[1<<i][i]=0 for all starting vertices i. Then we iterate over all masks from 1 to (1<<n)-1. For each mask, for each ending vertex i where mask has bit i set, we try to extend the path by adding an unvisited vertex j. The transition is: dp[mask|(1<<j)][j] = min(dp[mask|(1<<j)][j], dp[mask][i] + dist[i][j]). After filling DP, the answer is min(dp[(1<<n)-1][i]) over all i. Intuition: Instead of storing full paths, we store optimal costs for subsets, building solutions incrementally. Time: O(n² * 2ⁿ) from nested loops over masks (2ⁿ), i (n), and j (n). Space: O(n * 2ⁿ) for DP table. Connection to L1: This uses the DP transition as the key subroutine. Common mistakes: Not handling large n (n≤20 due to 2ⁿ memory), incorrect bit operations, missing initialization. Real code pattern: Initialize INF, dp[mask][i]=INF, dp[1<<i][i]=0. For mask in range(1<<n): for i in range(n): if mask&(1<<i): for j in range(n): if not mask&(1<<j): dp[mask|(1<<j)][j]=min(...). Answer = min(dp[(1<<n)-1][i]).

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Transition for Extending Paths
ladders.desc: Implement the core DP transition that extends a path by adding one unvisited vertex.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine updates the DP table when extending a path from an ending vertex i to a new vertex j. Given dp[mask][i] (minimum cost to visit vertices in mask ending at i), we compute dp[new_mask][j] = min(dp[new_mask][j], dp[mask][i] + dist[i][j]), where new_mask = mask | (1<<j). Intuition: If we have optimally visited vertices in mask ending at i, adding j creates a new path ending at j with cost increased by dist[i][j]. We take the minimum over all possible i. This is the heart of the DP, building solutions from smaller subsets. Step-by-step: 1. Check if j is not in mask (using bit operation). 2. Compute new_mask. 3. Update dp[new_mask][j] with the new candidate cost. Time per transition: O(1). Overall in full problem, called O(n² * 2ⁿ) times. Connection to L0: This transition is used in the main loops over masks and vertices. Common mistakes: Forgetting to check if j is unvisited, incorrect bit masking, not taking min. Real code pattern: if not (mask & (1<<j)): new_mask = mask | (1<<j); dp[new_mask][j] = min(dp[new_mask][j], dp[mask][i] + dist[i][j]).

examples.input: mask=3 (binary 011, vertices 0 and 1 visited), i=1, j=2, dp[mask][i]=10, dist[1][2]=5
examples.trace: Check j=2: mask=3 has bit 2=0, so j unvisited. new_mask = 3 | (1<<2) = 3 | 4 = 7 (binary 111). Candidate cost = dp[3][1] + dist[1][2] = 10 + 5 = 15. Update dp[7][2] = min(current value, 15). If dp[7][2] was INF, it becomes 15.

examples.input: mask=5 (binary 101, vertices 0 and 2 visited), i=0, j=1, dp[mask][i]=8, dist[0][1]=3
examples.trace: Check j=1: mask=5 has bit 1=0, so unvisited. new_mask = 5 | (1<<1) = 5 | 2 = 7 (111). Candidate cost = 8 + 3 = 11. Update dp[7][1] = min(INF, 11) = 11.

testCases.expected: dp[15][0]=90 (but j already visited, so no update)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Bitmask Representation of Vertex Subsets
ladders.desc: Use bitmasks to efficiently represent and manipulate subsets of vertices.
ladders.explanation: A bitmask is an integer where each bit represents whether a vertex is included (1) or not (0). For n vertices, we use n-bit masks from 0 to (1<<n)-1. This allows O(1) subset operations and compact storage. Intuition: Instead of storing lists of visited vertices, we use a single integer, enabling fast DP over all subsets. Key operations: 1. Set bit i: mask | (1<<i). 2. Check if bit i is set: mask & (1<<i) != 0. 3. Add vertex j to mask: mask | (1<<j). In DP, dp[mask][i] means we have visited vertices in mask ending at i. We iterate masks in increasing order to ensure smaller subsets are processed first. Time: O(2ⁿ) masks. Space: O(n * 2ⁿ) for DP. Connection to L1: Bitmasks define the mask parameter in the transition. Common mistakes: Off-by-one in bit positions (0-indexed), not handling large n (n≤20 for 32-bit ints). Real code pattern: for mask in range(1<<n): for i in range(n): if mask & (1<<i): # i in mask.

examples.trace: Bit positions: bit 0 for vertex 0, bit 1 for vertex 1, bit 2 for vertex 2. Mask 1: binary 001, bit 0=1 so vertex 0 included. Mask 3: binary 011, bits 0 and 1=1 so vertices 0 and 1 included. To add vertex 2 to mask 3: 3 | (1<<2) = 3 | 4 = 7. To check if vertex 1 in mask 5: 5 & (1<<1) = 5 & 2 = 0, so not included.

Vertices: 0,2,3. Bit positions: 0,1,2,3. Set bits: bit 0=1, bit 2=1, bit 3=1. Mask = (1<<0) | (1<<2) | (1<<3) = 1 | 4 | 8 = 13. Binary: 1101. Check vertex 1: 13 & (1<<1) = 13 & 2 = 0, so not in subset.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Over All Subsets of a Set
ladders.desc: Generate all possible subsets of n elements using bitmask iteration.
ladders.explanation: To solve problems involving subsets, we need to consider all 2ⁿ subsets. Using bitmasks, we iterate from 0 to (1<<n)-1, where each integer represents a subset via its bits. Intuition: Each bit corresponds to an element; 1 means included, 0 means excluded. All integers from 0 to 2ⁿ-1 cover all combinations. Step-by-step: 1. Let n be number of elements. 2. Total subsets = 2ⁿ. 3. For mask in range(1<<n): process subset where element i is included if mask & (1<<i) != 0. Time: O(2ⁿ) to iterate. Space: O(1) for iteration. Connection to L2: This iteration is used to loop over masks in DP. Common mistakes: Starting from 1 instead of 0 (missing empty set), incorrect upper bound. Real code pattern: for mask in range(1<<n): # do something with subset represented by mask.

examples.trace: n=2, 1<<n=4. Masks 0 to 3. Mask 0: binary 00, bits 0 and 1=0, subset {}. Mask 1: 01, bit 0=1, subset {A}. Mask 2: 10, bit 1=1, subset {B}. Mask 3: 11, bits 0 and 1=1, subset {A,B}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bit Manipulation for Set Operations
ladders.desc: Perform basic set operations (add element, check membership) using bitwise operators.
ladders.explanation: Bitwise operators allow efficient set operations on bitmasks. Key operations: 1. Add element i: mask | (1<<i). OR operator sets bit i to 1. 2. Check if element i is in set: (mask & (1<<i)) != 0. AND operator checks bit i. 3. Remove element i: mask & ~(1<<i). AND with NOT clears bit i. Intuition: Bits represent elements; operations manipulate these bits directly. Step-by-step: For n elements, use integers up to 2ⁿ-1. Example: mask=5 (binary 101), elements 0 and 2. Add element 1: 5 | (1<<1) = 5 | 2 = 7 (111). Check element 2: 5 & (1<<2) = 5 & 4 = 4 ≠ 0, so yes. Time: O(1) per operation. Space: O(1). Connection to L3: These operations are used when iterating subsets. Common mistakes: Using wrong shift, not using parentheses. Real code pattern: add: mask | (1<<i); check: mask & (1<<i); remove: mask & ~(1<<i).

examples.trace: Mask 3 has bits 0 and 1 set. Add i=2: 3 | (1<<2) = 3 | 4 = 7 (binary 111). Check i=2: 3 & (1<<2) = 3 & 4 = 0, so false. Remove i=2: 3 & ~(1<<2) = 3 & ~4 = 3 & -5 = 3, unchanged since bit 2 was 0.
Mask 6 has bits 1 and 2 set. Add i=1: 6 | (1<<1) = 6 | 2 = 6 (unchanged). Check i=1: 6 & (1<<1) = 6 & 2 = 2 ≠ 0, so true. Remove i=1: 6 & ~(1<<1) = 6 & ~2 = 6 & -3 = 4 (binary 100).

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Binary Numbers and Bitwise Operators
ladders.desc: Understand binary representation and basic bitwise operations (AND, OR, shift).
ladders.explanation: Binary numbers use base-2, with digits 0 and 1. Each digit is a bit. For example, decimal 5 is binary 101 (1*4 + 0*2 + 1*1). Bitwise operators work on bits: 1. AND (&): 1 if both bits 1, else 0. E.g., 5 & 3 = 101 & 011 = 001 = 1. 2. OR (|): 1 if either bit 1, else 0. E.g., 5 | 3 = 101 | 011 = 111 = 7. 3. Left shift (<<): Shifts bits left, equivalent to multiplying by 2. E.g., 1 << 2 = 100 = 4. Intuition: Bits can represent states (e.g., on/off), and operators manipulate these states efficiently. Step-by-step: Convert decimal to binary, apply operator bit-by-bit. Time: O(1) for fixed-size integers. Space: O(1). Connection to L4: These are the building blocks for bitmask operations. Common mistakes: Confusing bitwise and logical operators, not understanding shift. Real code pattern: a & b, a | b, a << b.
